title: Chicken Tikka Masala (British Curry House Style)

ingredients.name: boneless chicken, cut into 4 cm cubes

subs.name: paneer (400 g, cubed and pan-fried until golden) or firm tofu — reduce simmering time to 5 minutes
subs.notes: the sauce works beautifully with both

subs.name: thick coconut milk (100 ml) for the marinade — gives a different but still good result
subs.notes: the char will be slightly less vivid

ingredients.name: Kashmiri red chilli powder (for colour and mild heat)

subs.name: 200 ml canned crushed tomatoes or 3 tbsp tomato paste thinned with 150 ml water
subs.notes: paste gives a more concentrated, darker sauce

subs.name: cashew cream (soak 15 cashews in warm water 20 min, blend smooth with 100 ml water) — gives the same richness without dairy

steps.text: Mix the curd, half the ginger-garlic paste, half the red chilli powder, half the cumin, half the coriander and turmeric with salt. Add the chicken cubes, coat well and marinate for at least 20 minutes at room temperature — or overnight in the fridge for the best flavour.

Heat a tawa or skillet on high flame until very hot. Arrange the marinated chicken pieces in a single layer (in batches if needed) and cook for 3–4 minutes without moving until charred at the edges. Flip and cook 2 more minutes until cooked through. The char is essential for the tikka flavour — don't skip this step. Set the cooked chicken aside.
In the same pan or a kadai, heat oil and butter together over medium flame. Add the onion and cook for 7–8 minutes, stirring occasionally, until deeply golden but not burnt.

Add the remaining ginger-garlic paste and cook for 2 minutes until the raw smell goes. Add the remaining red chilli powder, cumin, coriander and garam masala and fry the masala for 1 minute, stirring constantly.

Pour in the blended tomatoes, add salt and cook on medium-high, stirring regularly, for 10–12 minutes until the tomato raw smell has gone completely and the masala is thick and the oil begins to separate at the edges of the pan.
Pour in the cream and stir to combine. The sauce will turn a rich orange. Add 100 ml water if the sauce is too thick — it should coat the back of a spoon without being gluey.
Add the seared chicken pieces to the sauce. Simmer on low for 8–10 minutes so the chicken absorbs the flavour. Taste for salt, garnish with fresh coriander and serve with naan, rice or chapatti.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: cream → cashew cream (soak 15 cashews in warm water 20 min, blend smooth with 100 ml water) — gives the same richness without dairy (a genuinely good dairy-free option); chicken → paneer (400 g, cubed and pan-fried until golden) or firm tofu — reduce simmering time to 5 minutes (the sauce works beautifully with both); curd → thick coconut milk (100 ml) for the marinade — gives a different but still good result (the char will be slightly less vivid); tomato → 200 ml canned crushed tomatoes or 3 tbsp tomato paste thinned with 150 ml water (paste gives a more concentrated, darker sauce)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: This dish is inherently suited to an Indian kitchen — the two-step tikka-then-curry method is standard Indian cooking. Use Kashmiri red chilli powder for the vivid orange-red colour without excessive heat (it is the British curry house trick). A tawa works perfectly for the charring step; finish the sauce in a kadai. Amul fresh cream gives the authentic restaurant richness.
